title: My Mother, The Car

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Michael Bluth was fed up with his mother's extravagant spending.' Cut to Lucille asking for a yacht crew to throw the most lavish party this town has ever seen.
jokes.setup: Narrator frames Michael as trying to cut back on spending.
jokes.punchline: Lucille immediately reveals she wants a yacht crew for the most lavish party this town has ever seen — the opposite of cutting back.
jokes.explanation: A classic AD structural joke: the narrator sets up an expectation that the scene immediately obliterates. Strong character integration for Lucille — this is entirely her brand of oblivious excess. Not a standout but efficiently sets the episode's comic engine.

jokes.text: 'To throw the most lavish party this town has ever seen for my birthday.'
jokes.setup: Michael has been explaining financial cuts and responsible budgeting.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's response is entirely self-absorbed and ignores everything Michael said — delivered with total earnestness.
jokes.explanation: Pure Lucille character comedy — the joke is in the complete disconnect from any awareness of Michael's point. The grandiosity ('most lavish party this town has ever seen') is perfectly calibrated to her self-image.

jokes.text: 'I enjoy bicentennials as much as the next guy, Mom, but we're not doing that.'
jokes.setup: Lucille wants a lavish party for her birthday.
jokes.punchline: Michael treats her desire for a 'lavish' party as equivalent to a once-in-200-years civic event, using absurd hyperbole to deflate it.
jokes.explanation: Clever and economical. The bicentennial comparison is a perfect absurdist escalation of the 'lavish party' concept. It's a sharp Michael line that plays on his weary practicality. High quotability.

jokes.text: Narrator describes Buster accidentally flirting with Lucille 2 while not wearing glasses, then accidentally bidding $10,000 on her at a charity auction, then accidentally entering her apartment thinking it was his mother's.
jokes.setup: Buster is avoiding Lucille Austero (Lucille 2).
jokes.punchline: The three 'accidents' escalate in absurdity and romantic implication, each one more committed than the last — accidentally entering her apartment saying 'I need you right now.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest structural jokes. The triple-escalation of Buster's 'accidents' is brilliantly built: each one is worse and more mortifying than the last, and all delivered via narrator with the same dry tone. 'I need you right now / Fellas, take a powder' implies Lucille 2 had guests and Buster burst in declaring need — deeply embarrassing and perfectly in character. The rule of three is used masterfully here with genuine escalation rather than a lazy third beat.

jokes.text: 'I need you right now. Fellas, take a powder.'
jokes.setup: Buster accidentally entered Lucille 2's apartment thinking it was his mother's.
jokes.punchline: 'I need you right now' — romantic/desperate — followed by Lucille 2 apparently having guests she must dismiss ('Fellas, take a powder'), making the intrusion even more mortifying.
jokes.explanation: The 'Fellas, take a powder' response is funny on its own — the archaic phrasing Lucille 2 uses implies she has male company she must shoo away. The layering of Buster's neediness landing in that specific context is excellent.

jokes.text: 'Oh, here comes the 10:15 conniption, right on time.'
jokes.setup: Buster is described as having panic attacks; he's clearly anxious.
jokes.punchline: Lucille has catalogued Buster's anxiety attacks by time of day, treating his mental health crisis like a train schedule.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional line. 'The 10:15 conniption, right on time' tells us everything about Lucille's relationship to Buster's anxiety — she's so unsympathetic she's turned it into a schedule. 'Conniption' is the perfect word choice. High quotability. Strong character integration — this could only be Lucille.

jokes.text: 'Honest to God, Buster, it's like every little thing makes you seize up in terror lately. I have no idea where you get that from.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is criticizing Buster's anxiety and fearfulness.
jokes.punchline: She immediately, with zero self-awareness, demonstrates she has no idea she is the cause — a perfect ironic denial.
jokes.explanation: The joke's punchline is the sincere bafflement — she genuinely cannot connect her own controlling behavior to Buster's anxiety. This is peak Lucille characterization. The audience has just watched her run the 'conniption schedule,' which makes the irony hit even harder.

jokes.text: 'Get away from that stove. You're going to light your hair on fire.'
jokes.setup: Lucille just claimed she has no idea why Buster is so anxious.
jokes.punchline: She immediately panics at Buster near a stove, proving she is the anxiety source within seconds of denying it.
jokes.explanation: This is the instant callback that makes joke #7 land at its full potential. The structure is near-perfect: the denial and the evidence come back-to-back with no pause. 'You're going to light your hair on fire' is gloriously specific — not just 'get hurt' but a vivid, Lucille-specific fear. One of the episode's tightest joke constructions.

jokes.text: 'He's weak.'
jokes.setup: Lucille has just demonstrated she smothers Buster with anxiety-inducing overprotection.
jokes.punchline: Her summary verdict on Buster is delivered without any self-awareness that she manufactured the weakness.
jokes.explanation: Two words that encapsulate an entire abusive dynamic. Economy is nearly perfect — the brevity makes it land like a punctuation mark. The casual cruelty is very Lucille.

jokes.text: 'Speaking of weak, Mom, what do you got in there, gold bars?'
jokes.setup: Lucille calls Buster weak. Michael pivots to commenting on how heavy her bag is.
jokes.punchline: Michael jokes 'gold bars?' as an absurd exaggeration — but the audience will later learn it is literally gold (Krugerrands). The joke pays off retroactively.
jokes.explanation: This is quintessential Arrested Development: a throwaway joke that is literally true in hindsight. On first viewing it's a mild quip; on rewatch it's a perfectly planted clue. The rewatch bonus is enormous. The line also transitions naturally from Lucille calling Buster 'weak' — 'speaking of weak' is a clever connector. Among the episode's best constructed jokes precisely because of its layered function.

jokes.text: 'Protein bars for your father. He's pumping up in prison.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks what's in the heavy bag.
jokes.punchline: Lucille is sneaking protein bars to her imprisoned husband who is 'pumping up in prison' — absurdly mundane prison detail delivered casually.
jokes.explanation: Funny detail — George Sr. lifting weights in prison is an absurd image — but the real value is as misdirection covering the Krugerrand reveal later. Works fine on its own, much better on rewatch.

jokes.text: 'You're my third least-favorite child.'
jokes.setup: Michael says 'No more parties' and declares he can live with being disliked.
jokes.punchline: Lucille ranks her children's disfavor with bureaucratic precision — third least-favorite implies she has a full ordered list.
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. 'Third least-favorite' is the perfect formulation — not 'third favorite' but third from the bottom of the dislike ranking. The precision implies she has thought deeply about this hierarchy of disappointment. Extremely quotable, pure Lucille, and lands hard. One of the episode's most memorable lines.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Michael came home to find his mother's fourth least-favorite child.'
jokes.setup: Lucille just ranked Michael as 'third least-favorite.'
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately uses the same ranking framework to identify Gob — implying Gob ranks even lower in Lucille's esteem.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant immediate callback. The narrator adopts Lucille's ranking system and weaponizes it against Gob before we even see him, which tells us everything about Gob's status and Lucille's pathology simultaneously. The speed of the callback (literally the next line) is what makes it exceptional. One of the episode's best jokes.

jokes.text: George Michael walks into the yacht and finds Gob with two women in bikinis. 'Well, they got the Asian right. "Hotties" might be a stretch.'
jokes.setup: Gob is living on the yacht illegally. George Michael walks in to find Gob has company.
jokes.punchline: Gob has apparently made some kind of flyer or ad calling for 'Asian hotties' and critiques the results clinically — both the racism of the request and his disappointment are equally appalling.
jokes.explanation: Classic Gob sleaziness, but this one ages poorly and relies somewhat on shock value. The character specificity (only Gob would do this and be casually offensive about it) saves it from being merely cheap, but the earned_vs_cheap score reflects the reliance on Gob's awfulness rather than genuine wit.

jokes.text: 'Well, let's start with the little one.'
jokes.setup: Gob has two women on the yacht. George Michael is standing there.
jokes.punchline: Gob addresses one of the women to 'start' with — implying a sequence — in front of his young nephew, with zero filter.
jokes.explanation: Functional cringe comedy. The humor is in Gob's complete obliviousness to George Michael's presence. Consistent with character but not a standout moment.

jokes.text: 'I'm sure there'd be somebody else out there that would appreciate her.' / Narrator: 'In fact, Michael felt that man was himself.'
jokes.setup: Gob suggests Michael tell him to break up with Marta.
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'someone else' is revealed by the narrator to be Michael himself — he's subtly advocating for his own romantic interest in his brother's girlfriend.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's intervention here is excellent — it catches Michael in a self-serving suggestion he's trying to disguise as advice. The dramatic irony is well-constructed and character-specific.

jokes.text: 'Michael, do you have any idea what would happen if I were to visit prison? It would be pandemonium.'
jokes.setup: Michael suggests Gob visit their father in prison.
jokes.punchline: Gob genuinely believes his presence at a prison would cause pandemonium — he's that grandiose about his own celebrity/magnetism.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Gob delusion. 'Pandemonium' is the exact right word for his self-image — he doesn't say 'it'd be awkward' or 'I'd be mobbed,' he says 'pandemonium' as if his arrival at a federal prison would be chaos. Pure character comedy.

jokes.text: 'It'd just give Dad one more reason to think that I've got nothing to offer but my looks.' / 'Yeah, I got some of that... except he also didn't like my looks.'
jokes.setup: Gob worries his father thinks he's just a pretty face.
jokes.punchline: Michael notes he has the same problem — except George Sr. also didn't like his looks. Michael gets no credit at all.
jokes.explanation: Michael's self-deprecating correction is well-timed and reveals something sad-funny about the Bluth family dynamic: even the competent son gets no respect. Good economy, good character work.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Lucille had recently been featured on Fox's World's Worst Drivers.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay claims Lucille is the world's worst driver.
jokes.punchline: The narrator confirms this is literally true — she was on a Fox TV program about it. The absurd specificity of 'Fox's World's Worst Drivers' lands the joke.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on multiple levels: it confirms Lindsay's hyperbole as literal fact, it mocks Fox's programming, and it sets up the later accident perfectly. The specificity of 'Fox's World's Worst Drivers' over a generic show title is the right comedic choice.

jokes.text: 'She didn't. I dummied her up a new one. Not my best work though. She wanted to look 48. I nearly airbrushed her into oblivion. We ended up checking "Albino" on the form.'
jokes.setup: Michael can't believe Lucille got her license renewed.
jokes.punchline: Gob forged it — and the details of the forgery are their own escalating joke: she wanted to look 48 (she's much older), he airbrushed her 'into oblivion,' and they ended up marking her as albino on the form.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. Each element escalates perfectly: Gob forged it (surprising) → she wanted to look 48 (vain and delusional) → he nearly airbrushed her into oblivion (vivid and absurd) → they checked 'Albino' (the perfect escalating absurdist endpoint). It's also deeply character-specific: only Gob would do this and describe it with this clinical pride. 'Airbrushed her into oblivion' is an exceptional phrase. High quotability.

jokes.text: 'For Gangy?' (George Michael asks how he knew about the surprise party) / 'She called and invited us.'
jokes.setup: Michael announces they'll throw a surprise party for his mother.
jokes.punchline: Lucille already called and invited the grandkids — she planned her own 'surprise' party.
jokes.explanation: Good joke that reveals Lucille's manipulation — she's always ten steps ahead and has no genuine desire to be surprised. Also works as a structural joke on the whole 'surprise party' premise.

jokes.text: 'Come on. This is a Bluth family celebration. It's no place for children.'
jokes.setup: George Michael and Maeby ask if they have to go to the family party.
jokes.punchline: Michael's reassurance is itself the dark joke — implying Bluth family events are so dysfunctional they're genuinely not suitable for children.
jokes.explanation: Sharp line. The inversion — celebrating by saying kids shouldn't be there — lands well. It's both an accurate description of the Bluths and a self-aware joke about the show's premise.

jokes.text: Lindsay visits prison to tell George Sr. she's been undervalued for her intellect — but 'didn't get the reception she was expecting.' No heads turn in prison when she walks through.
jokes.setup: Lindsay believes she turns heads everywhere she goes because of her looks.
jokes.punchline: In prison, surrounded by men who haven't seen women in months, she still fails to attract any attention — the ultimate defeat of her vanity.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully constructed premise joke. The setup is perfect: if any place should validate an attractive woman's vanity, it's a men's prison. That it doesn't is the ideal comedic subversion of Lindsay's self-image. The narrator's 'but didn't get the reception she was expecting' does excellent work framing the visual.

jokes.text: 'Gob's got the charm, Buster's got the...' [long pause] 'High-fastening pants.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is listing what each sibling is 'known for' — Michael has brains, Gob has charm, Buster has...
jokes.punchline: After a long pause, neither can think of anything for Buster beyond 'high-fastening pants.' The pause IS the joke — it signals how empty Buster's existence is.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The awkward silence before 'high-fastening pants' is doing enormous work — it signals there is genuinely nothing positive to say about Buster. 'High-fastening pants' is the perfect absurdist detail: not a talent, not a trait, not even an achievement — just a sartorial quirk. 'Have you said that?' / 'No. I'm saying that now.' adds another beat. Extremely quotable. Could only work for Buster.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Lindsay was surprised once again that no heads were turned.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay's prison visit was meant to show she could generate intellectual respect, not just turn heads.
jokes.punchline: The narrator reduces her quest for intellectual validation back to the 'heads turned' metric — she still failed by that measure too.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's callback to 'no heads turned' is sharp — it ties the subplot together and adds a layer. The word 'once again' implies this is a pattern. Good economy from the narrator.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'George Michael was finally moving beyond his crush on Maeby and felt happy to have a cousin he could just see a movie with. It was a love between two cousins that the world thought was wrong. But it was the world that was wrong.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is described as 'moving beyond' his romantic feelings for his cousin.
jokes.punchline: The narrator editorializes that cousin romantic love is not wrong — which is a darkly absurd endorsement of incest framed as earnest narration. The joke is in the narrator taking a position.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's tone shift into advocacy for cousin love is perfectly deadpan and deeply wrong. 'But it was the world that was wrong' is the kind of line that gets quoted. The AD narrator has always been unreliable/editorial, but this is one of its more subversive moments. High quotability.

jokes.text: George Michael and Maeby try to get into a movie. They are carded and turned away. Cut to: they are watching the movie with an elderly audience after an old man used his AARP card to get them in as 'guests.'
jokes.setup: George Michael and Maeby are too young to see the movie 'Les Cousins Dangereux' (Dangerous Cousins — a French film, itself a meta-joke about their situation).
jokes.punchline: They get in anyway via an old man's AARP guest pass — suggesting the movie's audience is entirely elderly.
jokes.explanation: The film title 'Les Cousins Dangereux' is a meta-joke about George Michael and Maeby's actual relationship situation — they ARE the dangerous cousins. This joke rewards close attention. The AARP solution is absurd and funny in its own right.

jokes.text: 'Mom, you've already got two strikes on your record. You strike one more person, and it's technically a spree.'
jokes.setup: Michael is reluctantly driving Lucille while she suggests she could drive.
jokes.punchline: Michael uses 'strike' as a double meaning — the bowling/legal 'three strikes' law AND physically striking pedestrians with the car. Calling it a 'spree' (as in killing spree) is the dark escalation.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional joke. 'Strike' doing double duty (three-strikes law + striking pedestrians) is clever wordplay that is also character-specific and dark. The escalation to 'technically a spree' is perfect — it treats vehicular assault as a sports statistic. Very quotable. One of the episode's top jokes.

jokes.text: Buster recently brought home a large rock from an archeological dig but 'had yet to find a way to get it out of the car.'
jokes.setup: Lucille complains about 'Buster's stupid rock' being behind her in the car.
jokes.punchline: Narrator reveals Buster has an archaeological dig rock he brought home and simply left in the car because he couldn't figure out how to remove it.
jokes.explanation: A planted detail that pays off during the accident reconstruction — the rock is what hit Michael on the back of the head. On first viewing it's a mild Buster character joke (incompetent, can't even unload a car). On rewatch it's a well-laid piece of mystery furniture.

jokes.text: Surprise party #1: Michael arrives with Lucille at the restaurant. 'Surprise!' — and she was surprised that her family didn't show up.
jokes.setup: Michael has orchestrated a surprise party for Lucille.
jokes.punchline: Nobody came. The family who orchestrated the party is not there. The 'surprise' is their absence.
jokes.explanation: Well-executed structural joke. The twist on 'surprise' — meaning the family didn't show rather than the guest being surprised — is clean and lands well. Sets up the emotional beat of Lucille's humiliation, which then drives the next act.

jokes.text: 'What about you? Neiman's?' / 'Prison. Then Neiman's. But only because I'm going back to prison, and they've seen me in this.'
jokes.setup: Michael confronts the family about missing the party. Lindsay claims she was at prison.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay was at prison AND Neiman Marcus — but only went to Neiman's to get a new outfit because prison people have seen her current clothes.
jokes.explanation: Excellent character joke. Lindsay's justification — she needs new clothes because prison people have seen her outfit — is perfectly Lindsay: completely self-absorbed, treating prison visits as fashion events. 'They've seen me in this' could only be Lindsay. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Buster was so worried about running into Lucille 2 he never left his mother's apartment. Lucille 2 was waiting for Buster to emerge. They were in a mutual standoff neither knew about.
jokes.setup: Buster avoided the party out of fear of Lucille 2.
jokes.punchline: Lucille 2 was also waiting to attend — waiting for Buster to come out first. They deadlocked each other's social lives simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: A clever situational irony — both characters trapped by the same dynamic, neither aware of the other's parallel imprisonment. This is the kind of structural comedy AD does exceptionally well: the information is delivered through the narrator and requires the audience to hold both facts simultaneously.

jokes.text: 'What frail act got you this time, Michael? She couldn't open a jar? What, did a clump of her hair come out in your hands?'
jokes.setup: Michael explains he was moved by something Lucille said at dinner.
jokes.punchline: Gob's list of possible 'frail acts' — jar-opening failure, hair falling out — is both prescient of the fake-frailty plot and funny in its specificity.
jokes.explanation: This joke retroactively becomes more interesting — Gob is correctly diagnosing the manipulation before we know there IS manipulation. 'A clump of her hair come out in your hands' is a vivid, specific escalation. On rewatch, Gob's cynicism is validated.

jokes.text: At dinner, Lucille delivers her guilting monologue — 'It's dry' / 'I've been a horrible mother' — and Michael immediately capitulates.
jokes.setup: Michael was trying to get Lucille to understand that she needs to be nicer if she wants her kids around.
jokes.punchline: Lucille immediately turns the criticism into a performance of self-flagellation ('I've been a horrible mother') and Michael immediately folds, reassuring her. The manipulation is instant and complete.
jokes.explanation: The speed of Lucille's 180 (from inscrutable dinner scene to 'I've been a horrible mother') and Michael's immediate betrayal of his own position is classic dysfunctional family comedy. The dinner context — a single word 'dry' — is a great setup.

jokes.text: 'Well, you know she's been a horrible mother, right?' / 'You don't have to tell me. But what was I gonna say?'
jokes.setup: Michael has just told Lucille she's a 'great mother.'
jokes.punchline: Both brothers immediately agree privately that she is, in fact, a horrible mother — while having just told her the opposite.
jokes.explanation: Honest and relatable family comedy moment. The quick sibling agreement that they both know the truth — but neither will say it — captures a universal dynamic. Works well.

jokes.text: 'Um, is it okay if I do it for Mom and not you? 'Cause I really like Mom.' / 'We know.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks Gob to throw the party for him even if not for Mom.
jokes.punchline: Gob's sincere admission that he 'really likes Mom' — delivered with puppy-like earnestness — is funny because of everything we've just seen Lucille do. 'We know' is the perfect deadpan acknowledgment of Gob's Oedipal attachment.
jokes.explanation: Gob's sincere declaration that he 'really likes Mom' is both funny and slightly disturbing given the context. Michael's 'We know' is perfectly calibrated — it's not dismissive, it's resigned. Strong character integration for both.

jokes.text: Buster breaks up with Lucille 2: 'I've already got a Lucille in my life.' / 'I understand. That's healthy.'
jokes.setup: Buster is trying to end his accidental relationship with Lucille 2.
jokes.punchline: Buster's reason for breaking up is that he already has one Lucille — implying the two are interchangeable. Lucille 2's therapist-speak response ('That's healthy') makes it stranger.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Buster's reasoning — he can't handle two Lucilles — perfectly captures his mother-fixation without spelling it out. 'That's healthy' as Lucille 2's response is beautifully ironic: she's affirming a breakup based on him being over-Lucille'd. Both characters are pitch-perfect here. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: 'Am I needy? Are you sure I'm not needy? 'Cause I feel needy sometimes.'
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 says she's 'foolish and funny and needy.'
jokes.punchline: Buster denies she's needy. She immediately demonstrates neediness by asking three times in a row if she's needy.
jokes.explanation: Self-refuting joke executed perfectly. The moment Buster says she's not needy, she proves she is by immediately asking for reassurance about whether she's needy — twice more. The escalation structure is clean and the self-awareness she lacks makes it funnier.

jokes.text: 'You know, we're probably not that different, you and me.' / 'You and I. Is it "I" or is it "me"?' / 'Buster, it's us.' / 'Yes. It is us. Us.'
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 tries to connect with Buster emotionally before parting.
jokes.punchline: Buster corrects her grammar pedantically. Lucille 2 reframes to 'us' — which Buster takes not as romantic inclusion but as a literal grammar agreement. The long, mutual 'Us' echo is absurd.
jokes.explanation: Layered joke: Buster's grammar correction is both pedantic and character-perfect (academically trained but emotionally oblivious). Lucille 2's pivot to 'us' is meant romantically but Buster takes it grammatically. The shared 'Us... us' repetition descends into absurdism. Rich character integration.

jokes.text: Surprise party #2: Michael arrives again with Lucille at the restaurant. 'Surprise!' — and once again, no one showed up.
jokes.setup: The first surprise party failed because no one came. Michael has now re-done it.
jokes.punchline: The exact same thing happens — no one showed up again. The repetition and Michael's apparent inability to prevent the same failure elevates it.
jokes.explanation: The running gag of the failed surprise party works because the repetition is absurd — Michael tries again without solving the underlying problem. The episode's B-plot (family not showing up) continues to ironically mirror its A-plot premise.

jokes.text: 'You did that for me?' / 'I let you drive, didn't I?'
jokes.setup: Lucille is touched that Michael spoke up for her to the family.
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'proof' of his devotion is letting Lucille drive — an act of insane generosity given she's the world's worst driver.
jokes.explanation: Funny callback that also foreshadows the accident that's about to happen. Letting Lucille drive is treated as an act of loving sacrifice — which, given what we know, it essentially is.

jokes.text: 'Hey. Is that Gob? It's an idiot on a scooter at night? It's gotta be Gob.'
jokes.setup: Michael and Lucille are driving at night and spot someone on a scooter.
jokes.punchline: Michael's logic: idiot on a scooter at night = statistically must be Gob. The reasoning is both an insult to Gob and funny because it's probably right.
jokes.explanation: Efficient character joke. The syllogism — idiot + scooter + night = Gob — is both an insult and a narrative setup. The confident identification based on pure idiocy is funny. Highly quotable as a character-defining line for Gob.

jokes.text: Lucille leans out the window yelling at the scooter rider — turns out it's not Gob — and hits him with the car.
jokes.setup: Michael tells Lucille not to scare the scooter rider. She does it anyway. 'Mom, that is not Gob. Mom! Mom!'
jokes.punchline: Lucille strikes — literally — a third person with her car. The 'spree' joke from earlier pays off.
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the 'spree' joke. Lucille completing her hat trick of pedestrian strikes is both absurd and darkly funny. The physical comedy of Michael helplessly saying 'Mom! Mom!' while it happens is well-executed. Strong callback reward.

jokes.text: 'Yeah. I guess. How's my mom? She's so frail.' / 'I'm fine. I'm fine. I was thrown clear.' / 'I only care about you.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks if the man Lucille hit is okay.
jokes.punchline: The man Lucille hit asks about Lucille first. Lucille immediately declares she 'only cares about' the man — while having just hit him with her car.
jokes.explanation: The man calling Lucille 'frail' (she's not) and worrying about her is a nice inversion. Lucille's 'I only care about you' — the most manipulative statement possible from someone who just hit someone — is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: 'What did you give me?' / 'Your mother asked me to pump you full of... Children's aspirin.'
jokes.setup: Michael wakes up in a hospital-like setting and asks what he was given.
jokes.punchline: The setup implies something strong; the answer is children's aspirin — the most ineffectual medicine possible.
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection — the beat before 'Children's aspirin' implies a painkiller or sedative. The reveal is both anticlimactic (funny) and consistent with Lucille's penny-pinching or incompetence. Works well.

jokes.text: Michael can't remember the accident due to short-term memory loss. 'Scoot... Scooter. Was there a scooter involved?'
jokes.setup: Michael has memory loss from head trauma. He's trying to reconstruct what happened.
jokes.punchline: 'Scoot' triggers 'scooter' — his trauma-addled brain reconstructs the accident in real time through word association.
jokes.explanation: Cute word-association joke. The 'scoot up' → 'scooter' trigger is a nice bit of comedic writing. Functions as an engine for the plot's mystery reconstruction.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I can't believe I did this. My insurance rates are gonna go through the roof.' Lucille: 'That's not what matters now.'
jokes.setup: Michael has just been in a car accident, has memory loss, and is in a hospital bed.
jokes.punchline: His primary concern is insurance rates. Lucille tells him that's not what matters — she's the one who actually caused the accident and is now managing Michael's belief that he drove.
jokes.explanation: Michael's middle-management anxiety about insurance rates in the middle of a medical crisis is funny and in character. Lucille's 'That's not what matters now' is darkly ironic — she's consoling him for an accident she caused while allowing him to take the blame. Rewatch bonus for the conspiracy layer.

jokes.text: Buster and Lucille 2, in bed together. 'Buster, you remember when we were kissing last night? It was a wild, wild ride. But is this something we can do? Is this something society will allow?'
jokes.setup: Buster has just broken up with Lucille 2, but apparently they got together again after.
jokes.punchline: They're now apparently in a romantic/sexual situation despite just having broken up, and Lucille 2's concern is 'will society allow this?' — as if their age-gap romance is the transgressive issue when the real transgression (it being his mother's close friend/doppelganger) is ignored.
jokes.explanation: Funny beat because it ignores the ACTUAL weirdness (she's his mother's friend/name twin) in favor of worrying about a relatively normal age-gap concern. 'Wild, wild ride' applied to Buster is inherently absurd.

jokes.text: 'I don't care what other people think. My God, for the first time in years I felt like I was standing on solid ground. My panic attacks have decreased. I feel confident. I'm feeling proud even...'
jokes.setup: Buster is describing how the relationship with Lucille 2 has made him more confident.
jokes.punchline: The speech builds toward something — then Buster presumably falls or has some physical mishap (the scene cuts or he collapses), undercutting his claim of newfound confidence and pride.
jokes.explanation: Classic escalation undercut. The speech builds pompously toward pride and confidence and is immediately punctured by reality (implied physical failure). Character integration is strong — only Buster's confidence arc would be this fragile.

jokes.text: 'Rest up, sweetie. You gave me quite a scare.' / [Michael processes] 'Scare. Did somebody say, "Let's give him a sc..."' / 'What is the matter with me? Clumsy Clara.'
jokes.setup: Michael has partial memory loss. Lucille says the word 'scare,' which triggers his memory of the accident ('Let's give him a scare').
jokes.punchline: He's about to put it together — then Lucille immediately deflects with 'Clumsy Clara,' taking blame for whatever Michael almost remembered. The deflection IS the confirmation of guilt.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best-constructed moments. Lucille's word 'scare' almost cracks Michael's amnesia — and she immediately self-incriminates by calling herself 'Clumsy Clara' and deflecting before he finishes the thought. On rewatch, you can see exactly what she's doing: managing his memory recovery. The structure is nearly perfect.

jokes.text: 'Totally cool. Didn't feel a thing. You know, Mom, I'm crazy about this aspirin. I can't believe we give it to children.'
jokes.setup: Michael was given children's aspirin for his head injury.
jokes.punchline: He's apparently responding very strongly to the children's aspirin — implying he's either very drug-sensitive or in denial about his pain.
jokes.explanation: 'I can't believe we give it to children' is a great line — the implication that it's too powerful for adult Michael is absurd and funny. Good callback that pays off the children's aspirin plant.

jokes.text: Gob's yacht. George Michael visits to get fake IDs. A woman emerges: 'She's an Olympian... hopeful. Swimming and some coaching.'
jokes.setup: Gob has a different woman on the yacht now. George Michael notices her.
jokes.punchline: Gob describes her as an 'Olympian — hopeful' with the qualification 'swimming and some coaching' — implying she's not actually an Olympian but a coaching-adjacent hopeful. Gob's vagueness about her status is funnier than any specific detail.
jokes.explanation: 'Olympian hopeful' is a great qualifier. Gob is trying to impress but keeps undermining himself with qualifications. Classic Gob self-sabotage.

jokes.text: George Michael tells Gob that Michael can't use the phone or go anywhere — he's trapped at Lucille's. 'That's when Gob decided to take the yacht to South America for the summer.'
jokes.setup: George Michael innocently explains Michael is laid up and can't enforce anything.
jokes.punchline: Gob immediately interprets 'Michael is incapacitated' as his cue to flee to South America on the yacht — treating his brother's injury as an opportunity.
jokes.explanation: The logic is pure Gob: his brother's vulnerability is his opportunity. The specificity of 'South America for the summer' — so casual and immediate — is funny. Good character integration.

jokes.text: 'I gotta alert this boat's crew.' [Cut to crew's devastated reaction.] 'They'll be devastated.'
jokes.setup: Gob announces he needs to tell the crew about the South America trip.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's prediction ('They'll be devastated') is followed (or accompanied) by the crew's reaction — presumably thrilled or indifferent, not devastated. Gob thinks his crew adores him.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the gap between Gob's self-importance and the crew's actual reaction. Works as a quick visual beat.

jokes.text: 'I just jumped through a plate glass window on a date.' / 'Oh. Well, women love to laugh.'
jokes.setup: Dr. Miller has apparently just come from an extremely eventful date involving a plate glass window.
jokes.punchline: 'Women love to laugh' — treating self-defenestration as a valid dating strategy, delivered completely deadpan.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional throwaway joke. 'Women love to laugh' as justification for jumping through plate glass on a date is bizarre and perfect. It's delivered so casually that it takes a beat to land. Maximum economy — the joke is entirely in those four words. One of the episode's most quotable lines.

jokes.text: 'Nothing. Gob was just teaching me how to hit it with a hammer.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks what happened to Buster's head.
jokes.punchline: Buster's explanation — Gob was teaching him to hit his own head with a hammer — is so casually bizarre it implies this is a normal thing they do.
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist line. The extreme casualness — 'Gob was just teaching me' — makes it funnier than any elaborate joke could. 'Teaching me how to hit it with a hammer' implies there's a right and wrong way, which adds another layer of absurdity.

jokes.text: Michael pieces together the accident: 'Gob was there. Gob was on his scooter. I think I wanted to hit Gob.' / Lucille (Bluth): 'Yes. That is exactly what happened. You wanted to give Gob a scare.'
jokes.setup: Michael is reconstructing the accident via fragmented memory. Lucille is feeding him the version that makes her innocent.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's eager, rapid confirmation — 'Yes. That is exactly what happened' — is suspicious precisely because of its enthusiasm. She's coaching his memory in real time.
jokes.explanation: This is where the manipulation plot becomes most explicit without Michael realizing it. On rewatch, Lucille's 'yes, exactly' reads as obviously suspicious. Good structural work connecting the mystery to the character.

jokes.text: Buster: 'I think I do.' [Knowing why Michael wanted to scare Gob — implying he knows Lucille orchestrated it.] Then pregnant pause before: 'Thanks, Mom.'
jokes.setup: Michael says he doesn't know why he wanted to scare Gob. Buster says he thinks he knows why.
jokes.punchline: A long pause followed by 'Thanks, Mom' — implying Buster has figured out Lucille's manipulation but is too dependent on her to call it out, or is thanking her for some unspoken reason.
jokes.explanation: The ambiguity of 'Thanks, Mom' after a knowing pause is an excellent awkward silence beat. It's unclear if Buster is thanking Lucille for the manipulation (approvingly) or has simply decided gratitude is the appropriate response to any Lucille behavior. The pause is where the comedy lives. Strong economy and character integration.

jokes.text: 'It is obvious I'm not wearing a bra, right?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay wants to cause a stir in prison with her looks.
jokes.punchline: She asks if her braless state is 'obvious' — completely tone-deaf in a prison environment, obsessively focused on her own effect.
jokes.explanation: Character-consistent joke for Lindsay but not particularly clever. The absurdity of the prison context saves it from being entirely one-note.

jokes.text: 'I haven't even been remotely harassed.' / 'That is not what I'm paying for.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is distressed that she hasn't been harassed in prison. George Sr. responds he's paying to prevent harassment.
jokes.punchline: They are at cross-purposes: she WANTS to be harassed (as validation of her attractiveness); he's PAYING to prevent it. The dark comedy is in Lindsay's desire for prison harassment as a compliment.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The reversal is perfect: Lindsay wants prison harassment as a self-esteem boost; George Sr. is spending gold to prevent it. The parallel miscommunication — and the dark premise of 'I WANT to be harassed' — is both character-perfect and genuinely surprising. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: 'Have I lost it? Did I ever even have it?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay learns George Sr. has been paying to prevent her from being harassed in prison.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's existential crisis — 'have I lost it?' — reveals she was relying on prison harassment as proof of her attractiveness. The question 'Did I ever even have it?' is unexpectedly poignant and funny.
jokes.explanation: The existential 'did I ever even have it?' applied to prison harassment is a great comedy beat. It's self-aware enough to be funny and specific enough to be character-perfect for Lindsay.

jokes.text: 'Your mother snuck 'em in here stuffed in energy bar wrappers to keep me from getting strangled in the shower or worse.' / 'Stabbed?' / 'In a way.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. explains he has Krugerrands (gold coins) in prison.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. says 'or worse' than strangled. Lindsay guesses 'stabbed?' George Sr. confirms 'in a way' — implying the 'worse' is sexual assault, which Lindsay completely misses.
jokes.explanation: Dark and clever. 'Stabbed?' / 'In a way' is a beautifully economical way to imply prison rape without stating it. Lindsay's obliviousness to the subtext is in character. The 'in a way' carries enormous weight in two words.

jokes.text: 'But you keep coming back here, honey, and I'm going broke.' / 'That's all I ever wanted from you, Daddy. For you to spend money on me.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. explains his financial sacrifice to protect Lindsay.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's father-daughter revelation moment is not about love or recognition — it's that she's glad he finally spent money on her. The emotional breakthrough is entirely transactional.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and one of the show's great character moments. The entire Lindsay prison subplot has been building to what seems like a father-daughter emotional breakthrough — and it pays off as pure materialism. 'For you to spend money on me' is the perfect subversion: her deepest desire isn't love or respect, it's financial attention. Character integration is a 9.5 — this could only be Lindsay. Very high quotability.

jokes.text: Michael tells Gob to move back onto the yacht: 'What makes you think I'd want to now?' Gob reacts with suspicion to the offer.
jokes.setup: Michael, still concussed and being manipulated, generously offers Gob use of the yacht indefinitely.
jokes.punchline: Gob's response — 'What makes you think I'd want to now?' — is funny because he was literally about to flee to South America on it. His pride prevents him from accepting even beneficial offers.
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy for Gob — his contrarianism and pride mean he resists even things he wants if they're offered. Functional but not standout.

jokes.text: 'Wow. How hard did you hit that steering wheel?' / 'I actually hit the back of my head.' / 'The back of your head?'
jokes.setup: Michael is describing the accident while under Lucille's narrative about what happened.
jokes.punchline: Gob catches the inconsistency — if Michael was driving and hit his head, it should be the front, not the back. This is the clue that unravels the mystery.
jokes.explanation: Clever structural joke — Gob (of all people) is the one who notices the logical inconsistency. It's funny that Gob is the detective here, and it's well-structured as a mystery-comedy beat.

jokes.text: 'I was halfway to South America, but I couldn't let you get away with it, because we're brothers, Mom, and we kind of like each other.'
jokes.setup: Gob confronts Lucille on Michael's behalf.
jokes.punchline: 'We kind of like each other' — the extreme understatement of sibling love as motivation is both touching and funny. Also, addressing Lucille as 'Mom' mid-sentence after 'we're brothers' is a funny grammatical slip Gob doesn't notice.
jokes.explanation: The callback to 'kind of like each other' as the emotional climax is well-earned and touching while still being funny. Gob's fraternal feeling expressed as 'kind of like' is a perfect Gob register.

jokes.text: 'But "skiff" is appropriate, right?'
jokes.setup: Gob dramatically orders the yacht to 'turn this skiff around.' The response is 'we haven't even left the dock.'
jokes.punchline: Gob's follow-up — 'but "skiff" is appropriate, right?' — shows he's more concerned about using the right nautical vocabulary than the situation itself.
jokes.explanation: Wonderful character joke. Gob has delivered a dramatic command, been deflated (haven't left the dock), and his response is to seek validation for his nautical terminology. It punctures the moment perfectly and reveals his vanity. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Gob rushes back. Michael: 'What are you doing?' Gob: 'Hey. Open this door.' [Banging.] 'You've been set up.' Michael: 'There's no way you were trying to hurt me. You don't hate me. Mom hates me. You kind of like me.'
jokes.setup: Gob rushes to tell Michael he's been set up by Lucille.
jokes.punchline: Gob's version of brotherly reassurance: 'You don't hate me. Mom hates me. You kind of like me.' — damning with faint praise in both directions, but genuinely moving from a Bluth.
jokes.explanation: 'Mom hates me. You kind of like me.' is an outstanding line for the Bluth universe — the emotional ceiling is 'kind of like,' which is treated as a meaningful and moving statement. The escalating negatives ('you don't hate me... Mom hates me... you kind of like me') land the punchline beautifully. Only in this family does 'kind of like' constitute an emotional declaration. Highly quotable, excellent character integration.

jokes.text: 'Mom must have clubbed you with a champagne bottle or something so she could drive.' / 'Gob, how'd you figure it out?' / 'It's funny you should ask that...' / 'No. He didn't figure anything out.'
jokes.setup: Gob presents a theory about Lucille clubbing Michael to take the wheel.
jokes.punchline: Michael asks how Gob figured it out, seemingly impressed. The narrator immediately undercuts: 'No. He didn't figure anything out.' — deflating Gob's detective moment.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional narrator joke. Gob has a moment of apparent cleverness — his theory happens to be right — but the narrator immediately clarifies he 'didn't figure anything out,' meaning it was a lucky guess or coincidence. It's meta (the narrator corrects the character) and it perfectly preserves Gob's idiot status. One of the episode's best structural jokes.

jokes.text: 'Hey, big shot!' [Flashback to Lucille yelling out the window.] Which was behind the passenger seat. Which means that she picked you up and dragged you over to the driver's seat.
jokes.setup: The brothers reconstruct how Lucille framed Michael for the accident.
jokes.punchline: The rock from Buster's archaeological dig — planted early as a throwaway — turns out to be the weapon Lucille used, and she physically dragged Michael to the driver's seat. The absurdity of the logistics is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The payoff to multiple planted jokes. Buster's rock (established as a throwaway early in the episode) becomes the instrument of Lucille's frame-up. The absurdist logistics — frail Lucille knocking out Michael, dragging him to the driver's seat — are played completely straight, which makes it funnier. The structure across the episode is what earns the high score. Massive rewatch bonus.

jokes.text: 'Mom can't pick me up. She could barely pick up that bag of protein bars.' / 'Guess what? They weren't protein bars. They were gold Krugerrands.'
jokes.setup: Michael argues Lucille couldn't have moved him because she could barely lift the bag of protein bars.
jokes.punchline: The protein bars were Krugerrands — solid gold. The bag was actually extremely heavy, which means Lucille is much stronger than she appears. Multiple planted jokes pay off simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke by structure. Every planted detail converges: Michael's joke about 'gold bars' (which he meant sarcastically) was literally true. The 'protein bars' were Krugerrands, which means the bag WAS extremely heavy, which means Lucille IS strong enough to have moved him. Lindsay's prison subplot (the Krugerrands paying off George Sr.'s protection) connects here too. The structure across the entire episode is masterful — this is a 9+ moment because it's exactly what the anchor describes: multiple jokes paying off simultaneously, rewarding attentive viewers.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Mom, you and I were getting along so well. I was thinking so highly of you. How could you make me think this was all my fault?' Lucille: 'Because if you knew it was my fault, you would've turned against me. And I had finally gotten you on my side.'
jokes.setup: Michael confronts Lucille about the cover-up.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's confession is completely unapologetic and strategically rational: she manipulated Michael because it worked and she wanted his approval. Her honesty about the manipulation is somehow more disturbing than the manipulation itself.
jokes.explanation: Lucille's matter-of-fact explanation of her manipulation strategy is dark comedy at its best — she's not apologizing, she's explaining her logic as if it's reasonable. 'I had finally gotten you on my side' treats Michael's approval as a resource she was trying to capture. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: 'And Dad finally paid attention to my intellect.' (Lindsay celebrates that the Krugerrand scheme proves George Sr. valued her mind.)
jokes.setup: Lindsay has been desperate for her father to value her intellect over her looks.
jokes.punchline: The 'evidence' of George Sr.'s respect for her intellect is that he hid gold coins in protein bar wrappers — Lucille's scheme, not Lindsay's. Lindsay takes credit for something she had nothing to do with.
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to Lindsay's subplot. Her misreading of the Krugerrand situation as validation of her intellect is very in character — she's taken the wrong lesson from every event in this episode.

jokes.text: 'Mom, you and I were getting along so well. I was thinking so highly of you. How could you make me think this was all my fault?' / 'Because if you knew it was my fault, you would've turned against me. And I had finally gotten you on my side. You were sticking up for me. I don't even remember you ever doing that before.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is confronted with her manipulation scheme.
jokes.punchline: Her explanation is completely sincere and self-pitying — she framed her son for a car accident because she wanted him to like her. The logic is both monstrous and pathetic, which is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The reveal of Lucille's motivation is both comedic and genuinely uncomfortable. She's a monster who just wants to be loved — and she expresses it through increasingly extreme manipulation. The comedy is in the gap between the crime (framing her son) and the motive (wanting him to stick up for her). Very Lucille.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Stop lying. Stop manipulating. Just be nicer.' / Lucille: 'But...' / [long pause] / 'You're right.'
jokes.setup: Michael confronts Lucille and demands she be nicer.
jokes.punchline: After a long pause, Lucille concedes: 'You're right.' — which is the most shocking thing she's ever said. The pause before it does the comedic work.
jokes.explanation: The joke is entirely in the delivery and pause — Lucille saying 'you're right' is so out of character that it lands as a comedic shock. The pause 'But...' sets up that she's about to argue, making the capitulation funnier.

jokes.text: 'I'm a horrible mother.' / 'No, no, Mom. You're great.' / 'That's crazy talk.' / 'You're a fantastic mother.' / 'I can't believe what I'm hearing, Mother.'
jokes.setup: Lucille repeats her 'I've been a horrible mother' performance from earlier in the episode.
jokes.punchline: This time, instead of just Michael reassuring her, ALL her children compete to reassure her — including Gob and Lindsay who privately admitted she's been a horrible mother. The manipulation has been completely successful.
jokes.explanation: Perfect structural callback. Earlier we saw just Michael cave to this move; now all three children are rushing to reassure her. The episode ends with Lucille having successfully manipulated everyone despite being exposed. 'That's crazy talk' is a funny line for Gob. The circularity — she wins anyway — is both funny and slightly dark.

jokes.text: 'On the next Arrested Development': George Michael and Maeby sneak back into 'Les Cousins Dangereux.' The ticket collector tells an elderly AARP member that they 'will not be starting Les Cousins Dangereux until you leave.' The AARP man protests his card covers 'one and a guest.'
jokes.setup: 'On the next' segment continues the Les Cousins Dangereux subplot.
jokes.punchline: The AARP man insisting his card covers him 'and a guest' for a film called Dangerous Cousins implies the elderly man is also there with an inappropriate companion — mirroring George Michael and Maeby's situation.
jokes.explanation: 'You know what? I knew society would never accept this.' — the AARP man's exit line mirrors the Maeby/George Michael situation perfectly. The parallel is very well-constructed and rewards attentive viewers.

jokes.text: 'You know what? I knew society would never accept this. And they never will.'
jokes.setup: George Michael and Maeby are once again thrown out of Les Cousins Dangereux.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's declaration — that society will never accept their love — mirrors the narrator's earlier editorial that 'it was the world that was wrong.' The cousin-love theme becomes the episode's final comedic note.
jokes.explanation: Good closing callback for the subplot. George Michael's grandiose framing of his situation ('society will never accept this') is both funny in isolation and a callback to the narrator's editorial earlier.

jokes.text: TAG: Michael's insurance takes another hit. Michael is in another accident. 'Ow!' Then: 'I was definitely driving that time.'
jokes.setup: The episode has been built around Michael being blamed for an accident he didn't cause.
jokes.punchline: In the tag, Michael is in another accident — and this time he definitively confirms he was driving, making the distinction from the Lucille-caused crash clear and funny.
jokes.explanation: 'I was definitely driving that time' is a perfect closer — the specific, pedantic clarification that this crash (unlike the episode's central crash) was genuinely his fault. It's a sharp episode button that also seeds the next episode's insurance subplot. The economy is excellent and the callback structure earns it.

jokes.text: Michael's insurance teaser: car crash #2. 'Ow! I was definitely driving that time.'
jokes.setup: 'On the next' — Michael gets in another car accident.
jokes.punchline: 'I was definitely driving that time' — clarifying that unlike the first accident (where he wasn't), this one is his fault. The contrast between the framed accident and the real one is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy in the callback. 'I was definitely driving that time' resolves the episode's central mystery (was he driving?) by contrast. A good final punchline for the episode.

tmdb_overview: Michael loses his short-term memory, which Lucille uses to her advantage. Gob attempts to use the family yacht before Michael sells it. George Michael and Maeby attempt to see an R-rated movie about cousins having an affair. Buster and Lucille 2 take "the next step".
insight: Absurdist character comedy sustains 63 jokes across tight 24 minutes without standout peaks.
